IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles an important bottleneck—creating training corpora for NLG micro-planning from real KBs—and provides a reproducible semi-automatic pipeline that yields a more semantically and linguistically diverse dataset (DBPNLG) than existing crowdsourced resources (RNNLG). The finding that standard seq2seq models perform worse on DBPNLG implies the dataset can push development of stronger KB verbalizers and micro-planners, opening clear research directions. However, impact is somewhat tempered by the limited release scope (only six DBpedia categories) and no mention of released code, which may constrain immediate widespread adoption. | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical gap—creating realistic, semantically rich corpora for NLG micro-planning—by presenting a semi-automatic pipeline that yields DBPNLG, which the authors show is more semantically and linguistically diverse than the crowdsourced RNNLG and that off-the-shelf seq2seq models perform worse on it. Those findings are likely to change how researchers think about training data for KB verbalization and to spur development of stronger micro-planning and surface-realization models. The contribution opens clear new research directions (richer KB verbalizers, content-selection models) and will be useful to the community, although its immediate impact is somewhat limited by the current release covering only six DBpedia categories and the modest dataset size.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important bottleneck—creating training corpora for NLG micro-planning from real KBs—and provides a reproducible semi-automatic pipeline that yields a more semantically and linguistically diverse dataset (DBPNLG) than existing crowdsourced resources (RNNLG). The finding that standard seq2seq models perform worse on DBPNLG implies the dataset can push development of stronger KB verbalizers and micro-planners, opening clear research directions. However, impact is somewhat tempered by the limited release scope (only six DBpedia categories) and no mention of released code, which may constrain immediate widespread adoption.

Those findings are likely to change how researchers think about training data for KB verbalization and to spur development of stronger micro-planning and surface-realization models. The contribution opens clear new research directions (richer KB verbalizers, content-selection models) and will be useful to the community, although its immediate impact is somewhat limited by the current release covering only six DBpedia categories and the modest dataset size.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a clear flow from motivation through method, experiments, and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and the methodology steps are presented coherently (KB extraction, ILP content selection, staged crowdsourcing). Notation and experimental reporting (including hyperparameters and a range of metrics) appear consistent and well-defined, and figures/tables/examples are described as informative. Minor presentation issues remain: the release/code availability is not mentioned and the paper could more explicitly call out some implementation artifacts (seeds, splits) for full reproducibility, but overall the writing quality is good and readable. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a clear intro → method → experiments → conclusion flow, with the main contributions stated early (creating DBPNLG and the semi-automatic pipeline). The staged data collection procedure, evaluation metrics, and experimental comparisons are presented coherently, and figures/tables/examples support the claims. Notation and technical terms appear consistent and well-defined, and the writing quality is good, though a few methodological subsections (e.g., the ILP content-selection and some crowdsourcing clarification steps) could benefit from slightly expanded explanation for readers unfamiliar with those specifics.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a clear flow from motivation through method, experiments, and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and the methodology steps are presented coherently (KB extraction, ILP content selection, staged crowdsourcing). Notation and experimental reporting (including hyperparameters and a range of metrics) appear consistent and well-defined, and figures/tables/examples are described as informative. Minor presentation issues remain: the release/code availability is not mentioned and the paper could more explicitly call out some implementation artifacts (seeds, splits) for full reproducibility, but overall the writing quality is good and readable.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a clear intro → method → experiments → conclusion flow, with the main contributions stated early (creating DBPNLG and the semi-automatic pipeline). The staged data collection procedure, evaluation metrics, and experimental comparisons are presented coherently, and figures/tables/examples support the claims. Notation and technical terms appear consistent and well-defined, and the writing quality is good, though a few methodological subsections (e.g., the ILP content-selection and some crowdsourcing clarification steps) could benefit from slightly expanded explanation for readers unfamiliar with those specifics.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles data-to-text micro-planning and introduces a semi-automatic pipeline plus a new DBPNLG corpus, compares against established baselines (seq2seq with attention), and reports standard metrics (BLEU, perplexity) and detailed linguistic analyses (CTTR, LS, input-shape diversity). From a methodological-reviewer perspective the work addresses the right audience and uses expected evaluation standards, but there are practical reproducibility and scope concerns (code availability not mentioned; released data covers only 6 DBpedia categories and there was an 8.7% verification loss) that prevent a perfect fit without minor revisions or clarifications. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP venue: it contributes a new corpus and a semi-automatic framework for KB-to-text micro-planning (DBPNLG) that addresses a clear shortcoming of prior work (RNNLG's shallow, artificial inputs). The methodology (KB extraction, ILP content selection, staged crowdsourcing) and evaluation metrics (perplexity, BLEU, lexical sophistication, input-shape diversity) meet community standards and are appropriate for researchers building more linguistically robust generators. The analysis showing that off-the-shelf seq2seq models perform worse on DBPNLG demonstrates the dataset’s value for driving model advances, and the paper candidly notes pragmatic limitations (six DBpedia categories, verification losses), which is suitable for this venue and audience.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles data-to-text micro-planning and introduces a semi-automatic pipeline plus a new DBPNLG corpus, compares against established baselines (seq2seq with attention), and reports standard metrics (BLEU, perplexity) and detailed linguistic analyses (CTTR, LS, input-shape diversity). From a methodological-reviewer perspective the work addresses the right audience and uses expected evaluation standards, but there are practical reproducibility and scope concerns (code availability not mentioned; released data covers only 6 DBpedia categories and there was an 8.7% verification loss) that prevent a perfect fit without minor revisions or clarifications.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP venue: it contributes a new corpus and a semi-automatic framework for KB-to-text micro-planning (DBPNLG) that addresses a clear shortcoming of prior work (RNNLG's shallow, artificial inputs). The methodology (KB extraction, ILP content selection, staged crowdsourcing) and evaluation metrics (perplexity, BLEU, lexical sophistication, input-shape diversity) meet community standards and are appropriate for researchers building more linguistically robust generators. The analysis showing that off-the-shelf seq2seq models perform worse on DBPNLG demonstrates the dataset’s value for driving model advances, and the paper candidly notes pragmatic limitations (six DBpedia categories, verification losses), which is suitable for this venue and audience.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable, practical contribution: a semi-automatic pipeline that combines KB-driven extraction (category graphs, learned property bigrams, ILP content selection) with a staged crowdsourcing protocol to produce a DBPNLG corpus tailored for micro-planning. This approach clearly differentiates itself from prior crowdsourced depth-1 datasets (e.g., RNNLG) and yields empirically more semantically and linguistically diverse data that challenges standard seq2seq models. The components are largely assembled from existing techniques, so the work is not a radical methodological breakthrough, but it is a meaningful and well-differentiated engineering advance that would inform experts building NLG corpora. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated contribution: a semi-automatic pipeline for creating data-to-text corpora from RDF KBs (DBpedia) that combines category-graph driven unit extraction, learned property-bigram models, and an ILP content-selection step plus staged crowdsourcing—this goes beyond prior crowd-sourced, depth-1 datasets like RNNLG. Demonstrating that DBPNLG yields greater semantic and linguistic diversity and is more challenging for standard seq2seq models is a substantive methodological and empirical advance. While the components individually are not wholly novel, their integration to target KB verbalization at scale and the resulting corpus represent a meaningful, non-trivial extension that would inform experts and drive new generator research.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable, practical contribution: a semi-automatic pipeline that combines KB-driven extraction (category graphs, learned property bigrams, ILP content selection) with a staged crowdsourcing protocol to produce a DBPNLG corpus tailored for micro-planning. This approach clearly differentiates itself from prior crowdsourced depth-1 datasets (e.g., RNNLG) and yields empirically more semantically and linguistically diverse data that challenges standard seq2seq models. The components are largely assembled from existing techniques, so the work is not a radical methodological breakthrough, but it is a meaningful and well-differentiated engineering advance that would inform experts building NLG corpora.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated contribution: a semi-automatic pipeline for creating data-to-text corpora from RDF KBs (DBpedia) that combines category-graph driven unit extraction, learned property-bigram models, and an ILP content-selection step plus staged crowdsourcing—this goes beyond prior crowd-sourced, depth-1 datasets like RNNLG. Demonstrating that DBPNLG yields greater semantic and linguistic diversity and is more challenging for standard seq2seq models is a substantive methodological and empirical advance. While the components individually are not wholly novel, their integration to target KB verbalization at scale and the resulting corpus represent a meaningful, non-trivial extension that would inform experts and drive new generator research.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and provides empirical evidence: a semi-automatic pipeline producing DBPNLG, corpus analyses (CTTR, LS, input-shape diversity, etc.) showing greater linguistic/semantic diversity than RNNLG, and worse off-the-shelf seq2seq performance indicating increased difficulty. The experimental methodology appears reasonable (comparisons to an established seq2seq baseline, reported hyperparameters, a mix of corpus-level metrics), but I see missing rigor in some controls—no mention of released code/seeded splits for reproducibility, limited coverage (only 6 DBpedia categories in the release), and no reported statistical significance or ablation studies isolating contributions of pipeline components. These are minor but important gaps; they do not overturn the presented evidence that DBPNLG is more challenging, but they limit confidence in generality and reproducibility. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: they build DBPNLG via a semi-automatic pipeline and show via multiple metrics (BLEU, perplexity, lexical measures, input-shape statistics) that it is more diverse and challenging than RNNLG and that an off-the-shelf seq2seq model performs worse on it. There are no mathematical derivations to check. The experimental methodology is broadly sound with an appropriate baseline and relevant metrics, but the summary does not mention statistical testing or whether the single seq2seq baseline sufficiently represents strong modern generators, and the released data covers only six categories (a noted limitation). Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, though broader claims about driving future generator development would be stronger with additional baselines or significance analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides empirical evidence: a semi-automatic pipeline producing DBPNLG, corpus analyses (CTTR, LS, input-shape diversity, etc.) showing greater linguistic/semantic diversity than RNNLG, and worse off-the-shelf seq2seq performance indicating increased difficulty. The experimental methodology appears reasonable (comparisons to an established seq2seq baseline, reported hyperparameters, a mix of corpus-level metrics), but I see missing rigor in some controls—no mention of released code/seeded splits for reproducibility, limited coverage (only 6 DBpedia categories in the release), and no reported statistical significance or ablation studies isolating contributions of pipeline components. These are minor but important gaps; they do not overturn the presented evidence that DBPNLG is more challenging, but they limit confidence in generality and reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: they build DBPNLG via a semi-automatic pipeline and show via multiple metrics (BLEU, perplexity, lexical measures, input-shape statistics) that it is more diverse and challenging than RNNLG and that an off-the-shelf seq2seq model performs worse on it. There are no mathematical derivations to check. The experimental methodology is broadly sound with an appropriate baseline and relevant metrics, but the summary does not mention statistical testing or whether the single seq2seq baseline sufficiently represents strong modern generators, and the released data covers only six categories (a noted limitation). Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, though broader claims about driving future generator development would be stronger with additional baselines or significance analysis.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides a reasonable set of experiments (corpus statistics, linguistic diversity metrics, perplexity/BLEU comparisons to an RNNLG baseline and an out-of-the-box seq2seq model) that support the high-level claim that DBPNLG is more diverse and more challenging. However, there are important gaps for a methodology-focused evaluation: no ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contributions of the category-graph, property-bigram, and ILP steps; limited category coverage (only 6 released) that weakens generality claims; and reproducibility issues (code/data release not mentioned, and no reporting of seeds, splits, or statistical significance). Given these strengths and shortcomings, the experimental evidence is adequate but missing key controlled analyses and reproducibility artifacts to be fully convincing. | B(4): The experiments are broadly comprehensive: the paper constructs DBPNLG, compares it to RNNLG using multiple linguistic and generation metrics (BLEU, perplexity, CTTR, LS, input-shape diversity), and evaluates a standard seq2seq baseline showing the corpus is more challenging. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses of the semi-automatic pipeline components (e.g., the effect of the property-bigram model vs ILP content selection) are reported, and the released corpus currently covers only 6 DBpedia categories which limits generality. Related work is reported as thorough, and several limitations/failure modes (manual property clarification, 8.7% verification loss, model shortcomings) are explicitly discussed; hyperparameters are given but code release is not mentioned, leaving some reproducibility questions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a reasonable set of experiments (corpus statistics, linguistic diversity metrics, perplexity/BLEU comparisons to an RNNLG baseline and an out-of-the-box seq2seq model) that support the high-level claim that DBPNLG is more diverse and more challenging. However, there are important gaps for a methodology-focused evaluation: no ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contributions of the category-graph, property-bigram, and ILP steps; limited category coverage (only 6 released) that weakens generality claims; and reproducibility issues (code/data release not mentioned, and no reporting of seeds, splits, or statistical significance). Given these strengths and shortcomings, the experimental evidence is adequate but missing key controlled analyses and reproducibility artifacts to be fully convincing.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are broadly comprehensive: the paper constructs DBPNLG, compares it to RNNLG using multiple linguistic and generation metrics (BLEU, perplexity, CTTR, LS, input-shape diversity), and evaluates a standard seq2seq baseline showing the corpus is more challenging. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses of the semi-automatic pipeline components (e.g., the effect of the property-bigram model vs ILP content selection) are reported, and the released corpus currently covers only 6 DBpedia categories which limits generality. Related work is reported as thorough, and several limitations/failure modes (manual property clarification, 8.7% verification loss, model shortcomings) are explicitly discussed; hyperparameters are given but code release is not mentioned, leaving some reproducibility questions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its new DBPNLG corpus against the relevant RNNLG prior dataset and evaluates a standard sequence-to-sequence baseline (Vinyals et al., 2015), and reports a wide range of corpus- and generation-level metrics—so comparisons are generally appropriate and carried out under comparable conditions. However, from the summary it appears stronger or more recent generation baselines (e.g., pointer/copy models, Transformer-based generators, or other KB-to-text systems) are not tested, which leaves open whether the observed degradation is due to corpus difficulty or model limitations. The authors do acknowledge when prior dataset-design choices limit applicability (Wen et al. 2016) and analyze differences in input shape and lexical diversity, and related work coverage is reported as thorough. Overall the comparisons are solid but missing a few contemporary baselines to be fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares its corpus against the leading crowdsourced RNNLG dataset and evaluates a standard sequence-to-sequence neural generator (Vinyals et al., 2015) on both corpora using multiple metrics (BLEU, perplexity, lexical and input-pattern diversity), which is appropriate and allows fair cross-dataset comparison. The authors acknowledge where prior data (Wen et al., 2016) is limited and analyze how those limits affect model performance; related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the experimental baselines are somewhat narrow (a single out-of-the-box seq2seq model) — stronger or more recent NLG micro-planning generators could have been included to strengthen claims that DBPNLG is more challenging in practice.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its new DBPNLG corpus against the relevant RNNLG prior dataset and evaluates a standard sequence-to-sequence baseline (Vinyals et al., 2015), and reports a wide range of corpus- and generation-level metrics—so comparisons are generally appropriate and carried out under comparable conditions. However, from the summary it appears stronger or more recent generation baselines (e.g., pointer/copy models, Transformer-based generators, or other KB-to-text systems) are not tested, which leaves open whether the observed degradation is due to corpus difficulty or model limitations. The authors do acknowledge when prior dataset-design choices limit applicability (Wen et al. 2016) and analyze differences in input shape and lexical diversity, and related work coverage is reported as thorough. Overall the comparisons are solid but missing a few contemporary baselines to be fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its corpus against the leading crowdsourced RNNLG dataset and evaluates a standard sequence-to-sequence neural generator (Vinyals et al., 2015) on both corpora using multiple metrics (BLEU, perplexity, lexical and input-pattern diversity), which is appropriate and allows fair cross-dataset comparison. The authors acknowledge where prior data (Wen et al., 2016) is limited and analyze how those limits affect model performance; related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the experimental baselines are somewhat narrow (a single out-of-the-box seq2seq model) — stronger or more recent NLG micro-planning generators could have been included to strengthen claims that DBPNLG is more challenging in practice.

main_claims: A semi-automatic framework can create data-to-text corpora from RDF knowledge bases (e.g., DBpedia) that are suitable for training wide-coverage NLG micro-planners.
The DBPNLG dataset produced by this framework is more semantically and linguistically diverse than the crowdsourced RNNLG dataset despite being smaller in size.
Existing sequence-to-sequence neural models perform worse on the DBPNLG dataset, showing that the resulting corpora are more challenging and can drive development of more powerful generators.
_summary.methodology: Extract varied, coherent data units from a KB (DBpedia) via category graphs, learned property bigram models and an ILP content selection; then crowdsource textual verbalizations in a staged process (single-triple verbalizations, merging for multi-triple inputs, and crowd validation) to produce aligned data-text pairs.
baselines: Multilayer sequence-to-sequence model with attention (Vinyals et al., 2015)
metrics: ratio of inputs to input patterns
limitations: Wen et al. (2016) dataset uses artificial, depth-1 inputs and cannot train KB verbalizers or promote deeper syntactic variety.
The presented DBPNLG release covers data collected from only 6 DBpedia categories (though more categories were collected for future work).
The crowdsourcing pipeline required manual clarification of unclear DBpedia properties and experienced an 8.7% corpus loss during verification.
Out-of-the-box sequence-to-sequence models are not optimal for generating linguistically complex texts from the richer DBPNLG data.

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML/NLP engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating data-driven generation systems and datasets; experienced in experiment design, benchmarks, and reproducible research infrastructure.
traits: insists on precise experimental protocols, clear baselines, and end-to-end reproducibility (code, seeds, data splits)
looks for ablation studies and controlled comparisons to isolate contributions of components
checks statistical rigor: appropriate metrics, error bars, significance testing, and dataset sampling biases
examines annotation procedures and crowdsourcing quality controls for potential sources of noise
values clear reporting of implementation details (hyperparameters, model checkpoints, compute costs)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the methodology and experiments support the claims, requests missing controls and reproducibility artifacts, and recommends concrete experiments or analyses to strengthen the paper.

reviewer_b.name: Broad Impact Expert
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with a track record in data-to-text, corpus creation, and resource-building for the community; monitors trends in dataset needs and how resources influence model development.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to existing datasets and corpus construction methods and situates work in the broader literature
assesses whether the dataset addresses real gaps (diversity, coverage, challenge) that will influence future research
considers downstream applicability, potential misuse, and ethical aspects of the data and collection protocol
values clear demonstrations of broader impact: who will use the resource and how it changes research directions
notices whether evaluation tasks and claims match community needs (e.g., micro-planning vs surface realization)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper on novelty, significance, and potential community impact, offering suggestions to better position the contribution and highlighting missing related work or applications.
